name: ISO/RTO
description: Operates the wholesale markets and the grid in a restructured region. Clears the energy, ancillary-service, capacity, and FTR markets and dispatches the system. Plays three roles: market operator, settlement administrator, and central counterparty — the buyer and seller for every cleared transaction.

name: Transmission Owner
description: Owns and maintains transmission assets. May or may not be the same legal entity as the operator. Recovers capital via OATT and revenue requirement.

name: Generators
description: Owners of physical supply — power plants, independent producers, storage, and hybrid resources. They bid into the wholesale markets and connect through the interconnection process. Distinct from LSEs (buyers) and traders (financial-only).

name: LSEs
description: Load-serving entities — utilities, competitive retailers, munis, and co-ops. They buy wholesale energy and capacity for their retail customers, hold the customer relationship, and carry obligations like state RPS targets.

name: Financial Traders
description: Participants with no physical assets or retail load — virtual bidders, FTR traders, and marketers. They take purely financial positions in the energy, FTR, and bilateral markets.

name: End Customers
description: Retail end customers — residential, commercial, and smaller industrial — served through an LSE under a bundled retail tariff. The whole bill arrives as one rate; they have no direct dealings with the wholesale market. Large flexible loads are split out separately (see largeload).

name: Large Flexible Loads
description: Large, often-flexible loads such as data centers, electrolyzers, and big industrial sites. Unlike ordinary retail customers, they can connect in different ways — through an LSE, directly at the transmission level, or behind the meter with on-site generation — and the connection type changes which wholesale charges they face and which instruments they hold. The focus of current debates over large-load cost allocation.

name: FERC
description: Federal regulator with jurisdiction over wholesale markets and interstate transmission. Approves ISO tariffs, issues orders, adjudicates disputes. Also has statutory oversight of NERC and approves its reliability standards.

name: State PUCs
description: State public utility commissions. Regulate retail rates, distribution, and (in vertically integrated states) generation.

name: NERC
description: North American Electric Reliability Corporation and regional entities. Sets and enforces reliability standards under FERC oversight (EPAct 2005).

name: State Legislatures
description: State legislative bodies and energy offices. Set policy goals via RPS, decarbonization mandates, RA targets.

name: DAM
description: Day-ahead energy market. Clears next-day hourly schedules and produces the hourly prices (LMPs) the ISO settles against.

name: RTM
description: Real-time energy market. Clears in 5-min intervals via SCED. Settles deviations from DAM positions at RTM LMPs.

name: AS Markets
description: Ancillary service markets — regulation, spinning, non-spinning, ramping. Co-optimized with energy in DAM and RTM.

name: Capacity Market
description: Multi-year forward procurement of capacity obligations (PJM RPM, ISO-NE FCM, NYISO ICAP, MISO PRA). CAISO and ERCOT use alternative mechanisms.

name: FTR Auction
description: Long-term, annual, and monthly auctions of congestion hedges. Cleared via simultaneous feasibility test (SFT) on a network model. FTRs are settled against DAM congestion.

name: Bilateral Markets
description: Over-the-counter contracts negotiated directly between two parties: PPAs, tolling, bilateral capacity, and financial swaps. No central clearing, but most settle against standard references like the day-ahead price.

name: Interconnection Process
description: The queue and study process for connecting new generation to the grid. Increasingly auction-like; allocates network upgrade costs and queue position.

name: ARR Allocation
description: Administrative assignment of FTR auction revenues to LSEs, based on historical load and transmission cost responsibility. Paired with FTR auction but mechanically distinct — no clearing, just a formula.

name: Transmission Tariffs
description: FERC-regulated transmission charges — the Open Access Transmission Tariff and related service rates. Sets what transmission users pay and what transmission owners recover; the ISO collects and remits.

name: Retail Tariffs
description: Retail rate structures set by state PUCs. Pass through wholesale energy, capacity, transmission, ARR credits, and policy adders. The administratively-set far end of the liquidity gradient.
